toc_entries.title: I. often wonder, I see him, some seeking time from him and those who are asked, finding him easy. Both of them desire this, because of which they seek time, but neither of them receives it; as if nothing is desired, as if nothing is given. The most precious thing of all is played with; yet they deceive them, because it is an incorporeal thing, because it does not come before the eyes, therefore it is considered the vilest, almost no price is attributed to it.

Li. fe is divided into three times: that which has been, that which is, that which is to come. Of those, what we do is brief, what we are to do is doubtful, what we have done is certain. This is because, in that to which fortune has lost its right, that which is not able to be reduced to the judgment of anyone. Occupied men lack this; for they have no leisure to look back at the past, and if they have, it is unjust to remember the thing to be regretted.

I. t is permissible to argue with Socrates, to doubt him Carneades, to rest with Epicurus, to conquer man by nature with the Stoics, to exceed the Cynics. Since nature itself is patient that all ages may accompany it in consortium, why are we, from this fleeting and fragile passage of time, to those things immense, eternal, which are common with the better, with such a mind, such a spirit?

L. et us say, those who are truly occupied with duties, who want Zeno, who want Pythagoras daily, and Democritus and other antistites of the good arts, who want Aristotle and Theophrastus to have them as the most familiar, no one of them will be idle, no one will reject the coming to him, happier, more loving of himself, no one will allow anyone to leave him without hands vacant; at night they can convene, during the day they can be free from all mortal men.

C. um videris itaque praetextam saepe iam sumptam, cum celebre in foro nomen, ne invideris; ista vitae damno parantur. Ut unus ab illis numeretur annus, omnis annos suos conterent. Quosdam antequam in summum ambitionis eniterentur, inter prima luctantis aetas reliquit; quosdam eum in consummationem dignitatis per mille indignitates erepsissent, misera subit cogitatio laborasse ipsos in titulum sepulcri; quorundam ultima senectus, dum in novas spes ut iuventa disponitur, inter conatus magnos et improbos invalida defecit. 2. Foedus ille, quem in iudicio pro ignotissimis litigatoribus grandem natu et imperitae coronae assensiones captantem, spiritus liquit; turpis ille, qui vivendo lassus citius quam laborando inter ipsa officia conlapsus est; turpis, quem accipiendis immorientem rationibus diu tractus risit heres. 3. Praeterire quod mihi occurrit exemplum non possum. S. Turannius[29
